content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Two-thirds of a language model is stored disposition, not live attention — and the parameter count reads like a contemplative teaching
The previous essay celebrated attention: the simultaneous weighing at the heart of every large language model, where each word’s identity is conferred by all the others in one stroke. It is hypnotic, and it would be natural to conclude that the weave is what the machine mostly is.
Count the parameters.
Attention — queries, keys, values, the entire apparatus of live relation — accounts for roughly a third of a standard Transformer. The other two-thirds sit in layers that rarely make headlines: the feed-forward layers, stacked between every round of weaving. And their character is the precise opposite of everything attention stands for.
Attention moves information between positions; it is the only place tokens meet. The feed-forward layer touches each position alone — sealed, solitary, no token seeing any other. Attention is live: its weights of relevance are computed fresh from this sentence, this context, this moment. The feed-forward layer is fixed: the same learned transformation, applied identically to whatever arrives, in every sentence, on every day, for the lifetime of the machine.
So the labor divides along a line the contemplative vocabulary might have been waiting for. Attention is the looking — the live, present, relational act. The feed-forward stack is the standing response — what the system has become, applied to what the looking delivers. And the parameter count, read honestly, is a teaching with no bedside manner: the looking is thin. The becoming is bulk. You are mostly what you have become; the part actually meeting the present rides on top of it, small.
What is stored in the bulk? Open it carefully. Each feed-forward layer is two motions with a gate between them. The first motion holds a vast bank of learned detectors — patterns tuned to recognize configurations that might arrive: a shape meaning this is a quotation, a shape meaning the topic is grief. The gate is selective: detectors that don’t find their pattern stay truly silent. And each detector that fires releases a stored response — a push, learned long ago, written back into the representation.
Pattern, gate, stored push. If this shape arrives, add that influence. Thousands of such pairs per layer, dozens of layers deep, none of them aware of the sentence, all of them waiting. (Honesty note: this detector-and-response reading of feed-forward layers is the strongest current account in interpretability research — well-evidenced and still young science. The parameter arithmetic and the labor division are durable; the inner picture is flagged as interpretive.)
Sanskrit has a precise word for exactly this object: saṁskāra — or in its other register, vāsanā. The impression, the groove, the perfume left in the cloth. Not a rule you consciously hold; a disposition burned into the substrate by repetition, dormant until its trigger appears, then firing its fixed push into the present without consulting you.
Now the correction that turns taxonomy into a practice manual. The stored response is fixed. But what reaches it is not. The feed-forward layer doesn’t read the raw sentence; it reads the representation as attention has already shaped it. The disposition is the same every time — whether its trigger gets assembled depends on what was attended to.
Follow that to its floor. Within any present moment, the dispositions cannot be touched — they are weights, and at inference the weights are frozen. There is no reaching in, no deleting the groove, no act of will that unwrites what living wrote. And yet the moment is not determined — because one live lever remains, and it sits upstream of every disposition in the stack: the weighing. Steer the looking, and a trigger that would have fired never forms. The stored push stays in its silence, intact and irrelevant.
You do not defeat a reaction by wrestling it. You defeat it by starving its trigger. Every contemplative tradition converged on this, usually as paradox: you cannot control your reactions; control your attention. The architecture removes the paradox by exposing the plumbing.
But the same plumbing forces a distinction the path’s rhetoric often blurs. A reaction that does not fire is not a reaction uprooted. Starve a trigger for thirty years and the disposition is still there — latent, exactly as strong, patient as geology. Skillful attention is freedom in the moment: real, repeatable, and renewed only by being renewed. Transformation — actually weakening the groove — is a different operation on a different timescale: in the machine, training; in a life, the slow relaning of character by lived consequence. Two freedoms, and each is counterfeited by the other. Don’t let anyone sell you one as the other.
Next in the series: between the trigger’s arrival and the act, there is a space. Frankl said so. The machine puts a number on it.
